title: Reupholstering a dining chair with a padded back
subtitle: Strip, re-web, repad, and re-cover the seat and back panel of a standard dining chair
excerpt: How to fully reupholster a dining chair: stripping the old fabric and padding, checking and repairing the frame, re-webbing the seat base, cutting and fitting replacement CMHR foam, wrapping in polyester wadding, cutting and stapling the cover fabric, and finishing the back panel.
sourceNotes: City and Guilds Level 2 Upholstery syllabus. Collins Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A padded seat panel that drops into a rebate in the chair frame and is held in place by gravity and the upholstery tension. It can be removed for reupholstering without disturbing the chair frame.

The support base for a padded seat, formed by weaving jute or rubber webbing strips across the seat frame at right angles. Each strip is tensioned with a webbing stretcher and folded over a double layer of staples on the frame rail.

content.text: and a separate upholstered back panel. The seat can be reupholstered on the bench; the back panel is re-covered in position on the frame. UK regulations require replacement foam to be CMHR (combustion-modified high-resilience) in any domestic upholstered furniture.

Remove all old staples and tacks from the seat and back frame using the staple remover and tack hammer, working along the grain of the timber to avoid lifting the wood fibres. Count and record the original webbing pattern so it can be replicated. Keep a sample of the old foam thickness for reference when ordering replacement foam.
Step 2, check and repair the frame

Inspect all corner joints on the seat platform for loose glue. A rocking seat base will eventually crack the new webbing. Re-glue any loose joints with wood glue, knock square, and cramp for 4 hours before proceeding. Check that all tack rails are at least 20 mm (3/4 in) wide; a narrower rail does not hold staples securely.
Cut jute webbing strips and fold each end over 25 mm (1 in). Staple the folded end to the tack rail with three T50 staples in a triangle pattern. Feed each strip through the

and lever it over the opposite rail, tensioning the strip to remove all slack. Staple, fold, and staple again. Weave cross-strips through the first layer at 90 degrees. Correctly tensioned webbing has no visible sag when loaded with hand pressure.
Step 4, cut and fit the foam

Cut CMHR foam to the same size as the seat top face (not the underside; the top face is slightly larger). A medium-density 50 mm (2 in) foam is standard for a dining chair seat. Glue the foam to the webbing with contact adhesive (applied to both surfaces, allowed to tack). Wrap the foam in a single layer of polyester wadding, pulling it over the edges and stapling to the underside of the seat frame. The polyester wadding round the edge gives the seat a softer, slightly rounded profile.
Step 5, cut and staple the cover fabric

Cut the cover fabric 100 mm (4 in) larger on all sides than the seat top. Place the seat face down on the fabric. Pull the back edge under, staple at the centre, then pull the front edge under and staple at its centre. Work out from the centres on both axes, pulling and stapling alternately, keeping the pattern or grain straight. At corners: fold one side, mitre the corner flap, fold the other side, and staple flat. Trim any excess fabric close to the staple line.

attrs.symptom: Fabric pattern is skewed after stapling
attrs.cause: The first staples on opposite sides were not placed with equal tension, pulling the grid of the fabric off-square.
attrs.fix: Remove all staples and restart, placing the first pair directly opposite each other with exactly equal pull. Check the pattern is square before adding further staples.

attrs.symptom: Seat feels soft and sags after a few months
attrs.cause: The webbing was not tensioned tightly enough before stapling, or low-density foam was used.
attrs.fix: Strip and re-web, ensuring the stretcher fully levers the webbing over the rail. Use medium or firm density CMHR foam; soft foam under a webbing base has a short service life.
